title: What is an IIFE?
answer: An **IIFE** `(function(){ ... })()` runs immediately and creates a private scope, avoiding global namespace pollution. It was the classic module pattern before ES modules and block scoping (`let`/`const`).

title: What is the difference between arrow and regular functions?
answer: **Arrow functions** have no own `this` (they inherit it lexically), no `arguments` object, can't be used as constructors, and have no `prototype`. Regular functions bind `this` based on the call site. Use arrows for callbacks where you want the surrounding `this`.

title: What does the 'new' keyword do?
answer: **new** creates a fresh object, sets its prototype to the constructor's `.prototype`, binds `this` to it while running the constructor, and returns that object (unless the constructor returns its own object).

title: What does Object.create do?
answer: **Object.create(proto)** creates a new object with `proto` as its prototype, enabling prototypal inheritance without constructors. `Object.create(null)` makes a truly empty object with no inherited properties — handy as a clean map.

title: How does instanceof work?
answer: **instanceof** checks whether a constructor's `prototype` appears anywhere in an object's prototype chain. It tests inheritance relationships, unlike `typeof` which only reports primitive/object categories.

title: What are the quirks of the typeof operator?
answer: `typeof` returns a type string but has quirks: `typeof null` is `'object'` (a historic bug), `typeof []` is `'object'` (use `Array.isArray`), `typeof function(){}` is `'function'`, and `typeof undeclaredVar` safely returns `'undefined'`.

title: How do you correctly check for NaN?
answer: `NaN` is the only value not equal to itself (`NaN === NaN` is false). Use **`Number.isNaN(x)`** for a reliable check; the global `isNaN` coerces its argument first (`isNaN('foo')` is true), which is misleading.

title: Why is 0.1 + 0.2 not exactly 0.3?
answer: JavaScript numbers are IEEE-754 doubles, which can't represent some decimals exactly, so `0.1 + 0.2` is `0.30000000000000004`. Compare with a small epsilon, or use integers (cents) / a decimal library for money.

title: How does Array.reduce work?
answer: **reduce(callback, initial)** folds an array into a single value by accumulating: the callback gets `(accumulator, current)` and returns the next accumulator. It can build sums, objects, groupings, or even reimplement map/filter.

title: What does Array.filter do?
answer: **filter(predicate)** returns a new array containing only the elements for which the predicate returns truthy. It doesn't mutate the original and is chainable with map/reduce.

title: What is the difference between find and filter?
answer: **find** returns the **first** element matching a predicate (or `undefined`) and stops early; **filter** returns **all** matches as a new array. Use `find`/`findIndex` when you need a single item.

title: What do Array.some and Array.every do?
answer: **some(pred)** returns true if at least one element passes (short-circuits on first true). **every(pred)** returns true only if all pass (short-circuits on first false). They answer existential/universal questions about an array.

title: What do flat and flatMap do?
answer: **flat(depth)** flattens nested arrays by the given depth (default 1). **flatMap(fn)** maps then flattens one level — useful for expanding each item into zero or more results in one pass.

title: When should you use Map and Set over objects and arrays?
answer: **Map** allows any key type (including objects), preserves insertion order, has a `size`, and is optimized for frequent add/remove. **Set** stores unique values with O(1) membership. Prefer them over objects/arrays when keys aren't strings or you need dedup/fast lookups.

title: What are Symbols and what are they used for?
answer: A **Symbol** is a unique, immutable primitive often used as a non-colliding object key or to define well-known behaviors (e.g. `Symbol.iterator`). Symbol-keyed properties are hidden from normal enumeration, giving a form of metadata/privacy.

title: How do iterators and the iterable protocol work?
answer: An object is **iterable** if it has a `[Symbol.iterator]()` method returning an **iterator** with a `next()` that yields `{ value, done }`. This protocol powers `for...of`, spread, and destructuring. Implement it (or use a generator) to make custom objects iterable.

title: What is the difference between for...of and for...in?
answer: **for...of** iterates iterable **values** (arrays, strings, maps). **for...in** iterates enumerable **keys** including inherited ones — best for plain objects, but risky on arrays. Use `for...of` for arrays and `Object.keys` for objects.

title: What do Object.keys, values, and entries return?
answer: **Object.keys(o)** returns own enumerable keys, **Object.values(o)** the values, and **Object.entries(o)** `[key, value]` pairs (great with `for...of` or to build a Map). `Object.fromEntries` reverses entries back into an object.

title: What is the difference between Promise.all, allSettled, race, and any?
answer: **all** resolves when all succeed (rejects on first failure). **allSettled** waits for all and reports each outcome. **race** settles with the first to settle (resolve or reject). **any** resolves with the first success (rejects only if all fail).

title: What is the difference between microtasks and macrotasks?
answer: **Microtasks** (promise callbacks, `queueMicrotask`, `MutationObserver`) run after the current task and **before** the next render or macrotask. **Macrotasks** (`setTimeout`, I/O, events) run one per loop iteration. So a resolved promise's `.then` runs before a `setTimeout(0)`.

title: What does setTimeout with 0 delay actually do?
answer: `setTimeout(fn, 0)` doesn't run immediately — it queues `fn` as a **macrotask** to run after the current synchronous code (and pending microtasks) finish. The minimum delay is also clamped (~4ms for nested timers).

title: What does 'use strict' do?
answer: **Strict mode** catches silent errors: it forbids undeclared variables, makes `this` `undefined` in plain function calls, disallows duplicate parameters, and throws on assignments to read-only properties. ES modules and class bodies are strict by default.

title: What is lexical scoping and the scope chain?
answer: **Lexical scoping** means a function's accessible variables are determined by where it's **defined**, not called. The **scope chain** is the nested lookup from inner scopes outward to the global scope — the basis for closures.

title: What are function composition and pipe?
answer: **Composition** combines functions so the output of one feeds the next. `compose(f, g)(x)` = `f(g(x))` (right-to-left); `pipe` runs left-to-right. They build complex transformations from small reusable functions, a functional-programming staple.

title: What are the logical assignment operators?
answer: **`a ||= b`** assigns `b` only if `a` is falsy; **`a &&= b`** only if `a` is truthy; **`a ??= b`** only if `a` is null/undefined. They're concise shorthands for common conditional-assignment patterns.

title: What are computed property names?
answer: **Computed property names** let you use an expression as an object key in a literal: `{ [keyVar]: value }` or `{ [`item_${id}`]: x }`. They're handy for dynamic keys and building objects from variables.

title: What are tagged template literals?
answer: A **tagged template** calls a function with the literal's string parts and interpolated values: `tag`Hello ${name}``. The tag can transform/escape the result — used by libraries like styled-components, GraphQL `gql`, and HTML sanitizers.

title: What is a Proxy in JavaScript?
answer: A **Proxy** wraps an object and intercepts fundamental operations (get, set, has, deleteProperty…) via trap handlers. It enables reactive frameworks (Vue 3), validation, logging, and virtualized objects without changing the original.

title: What is the Reflect API?
answer: **Reflect** provides functions mirroring object internal operations (`Reflect.get`, `set`, `has`, `ownKeys`, `apply`). It pairs with Proxy traps to perform the default behavior cleanly and returns booleans instead of throwing, improving reliability.

title: How does the fetch API work?
answer: **fetch(url, options)** returns a Promise resolving to a `Response`. You must call `.json()`/`.text()` (also promises) to read the body. Note: fetch only rejects on network errors, not HTTP error statuses — check `response.ok`/`status` yourself.

title: What is CORS?
answer: **CORS** is a browser security mechanism that blocks cross-origin requests unless the server opts in with `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` (and related) headers. Non-simple requests trigger a preflight `OPTIONS`. It's enforced by the browser, not the server.

title: What is the difference between localStorage, sessionStorage, and cookies?
answer: **localStorage** persists until cleared; **sessionStorage** lasts for the tab session; both hold ~5MB of strings and aren't sent to the server. **Cookies** are small, sent with every request (good for auth), and support expiry/flags like HttpOnly and Secure.

title: Why must you pass the same function reference to removeEventListener?
answer: **removeEventListener** only removes a listener if you pass the **exact same function reference** used in `addEventListener`. An inline or freshly-created arrow function won't match, so store the handler in a variable to be able to remove it.

title: What is the difference between preventDefault and stopPropagation?
answer: **preventDefault()** cancels the browser's default action (e.g. form submit, link navigation). **stopPropagation()** stops the event from bubbling/capturing to other handlers. They're independent — you may use one, both, or neither.

title: What is globalThis?
answer: **globalThis** is a standard way to access the global object across environments — `window` in browsers, `global` in Node, `self` in workers — so code can reference globals portably without environment checks.

title: What are private class fields?
answer: Fields prefixed with **`#`** (e.g. `#count`) are truly private — accessible only inside the class, enforced at runtime (not just by convention like `_name`). Private methods and static private members are also supported.

title: What are static methods and properties?
answer: **static** members belong to the class itself, not instances — called as `ClassName.method()`. They're used for factory methods, constants, and utilities related to the class but not tied to a particular instance.

title: How do getters and setters work?
answer: **get**/**set** define accessor properties that run a function on read/write while being used like a normal property (`obj.full`, `obj.full = x`). They enable computed properties, validation, and encapsulation in classes and object literals.

title: What is BigInt?
answer: **BigInt** represents integers beyond `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER` (2^53−1), written with an `n` suffix (`123n`) or `BigInt(x)`. You can't mix BigInt and Number in arithmetic without explicit conversion. Useful for large IDs, timestamps, and precise integer math.

title: What do Array.from and Array.of do?
answer: **Array.from(iterableOrArrayLike, mapFn?)** builds an array from anything iterable or array-like (NodeLists, strings, Sets), optionally mapping. **Array.of(...items)** creates an array from its arguments, avoiding the `new Array(3)` length-vs-element ambiguity.

title: What does yield* do in a generator?
answer: **`yield*`** delegates to another iterable/generator, yielding all its values as if inline. It's used to compose generators — e.g. a generator that flattens or chains the output of sub-generators.

title: What are async iterators and for await...of?
answer: An **async iterator** has `[Symbol.asyncIterator]` and a `next()` returning a Promise of `{value, done}`. **`for await...of`** consumes it, awaiting each value — ideal for streaming data like paginated APIs or readable streams.

title: How do you convert a callback-based function to a Promise?
answer: Wrap it: return `new Promise((resolve, reject) => fn(args, (err, data) => err ? reject(err) : resolve(data)))`. Node also provides `util.promisify` for the standard error-first callback convention.

title: How do you implement a throttle function?
answer: Throttle limits a function to once per interval: track the last run time (or a timer flag) and ignore calls until the interval elapses. Unlike debounce (which waits for inactivity), throttle guarantees periodic execution — good for scroll/resize handlers.

title: What are the basics of regular expressions in JavaScript?
answer: A **RegExp** (`/pattern/flags`) matches text. Common flags: `g` (global), `i` (case-insensitive), `m` (multiline). Use `test()` for a boolean, `match`/`matchAll`/`replace` on strings, and capture groups `()` (or named `(?<name>)`) to extract parts.

title: What is short-circuit evaluation?
answer: **`&&`** returns the first falsy operand (or the last if all truthy); **`||`** returns the first truthy operand. They short-circuit — stop evaluating once the result is known — enabling guards (`obj && obj.x`) and defaults (`x || fallback`).

title: What does the delete operator do?
answer: **delete obj.prop** removes a property from an object (returning true). It doesn't affect variables or, on arrays, reindex — it leaves a hole. For arrays use `splice`. Deleting can also deoptimize objects, so reassigning to `undefined` is sometimes preferred.

title: What is the difference between Object.freeze, seal, and preventExtensions?
answer: **preventExtensions** blocks adding new properties. **seal** also blocks deleting and reconfiguring (but values can change). **freeze** is the strongest — no add, delete, or change of existing values (shallowly). All are shallow.

title: In what order do console.log, setTimeout, and Promise.then run?
answer: Synchronous `console.log` runs first; then all **microtasks** (`Promise.then`) drain; then **macrotasks** (`setTimeout`). So `log; setTimeout(()=>log 'C'); Promise.resolve().then(()=>log 'B')` prints the sync log, then 'B', then 'C'.

title: What is the difference between includes and indexOf?
answer: **includes(value)** returns a boolean and (unlike `indexOf`, which returns an index or `-1`) correctly finds `NaN`. Use `includes` for readable membership checks; use `indexOf` when you actually need the position.
